## Section 7.1.4: field

**Summary:** This model uses a list of fields and burst values to determine if a patch has burst. The inputs can be given as a single entry, multiple burst conditions, or as a list of names and values.

**Content:**
A list of fields and bust values are used to determine if the patch has burst. The inputs can either be given as a single entry (”burstValue”), multiple burst conditions (”burstValues”), or as a list of names and values (”fields” and ”values”). The latter is used when writing to remove errors in ParaView. See below for an example.

**Parameters Table:**
[
  {
    "Variable": "burstValue",
    "Description": "Used for single field burst condition [name value]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "burstValues",
    "Description": "Used for multiple field burst condition [(name1 value1 name2 value2 ... nameN valueN)]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "fields",
    "Description": "Name of the fields"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "values",
    "Description": "Values at which burst occurs"
  }
]


---
## Section 7.1: Burst models

**Summary:** This section explains that the method for calculating when a burst occurs is runtime-selectable, with inputs placed in the constant/polyMesh/boundary file. The settings can also be added within the patch entry of blockMeshDict or snappyHexMeshDict.

**Content:**
The method to calculate where burst occurs is runtime-selectable and the inputs are placed in the constant/polyMesh/boundary file under the corresponding boundary. This can also be added in either the patch entry within blockMeshDict of snappyHexMeshDict.

**Parameters Table:**
[
  {
    "Variable": "partialBurst",
    "Description": "Can individual face burst (no by default)"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "useDelta",
    "Description": "Us the difference across coupled patches rather than the maximum value (yes by default)"
  }
]


---
## Section 7: Burst patches

**Summary:** This chapter describes the use of 'bursting' patches, including burst (external), burstCyclic (internal without AMR), and burstCyclicAMI (general internal). All fields for these patches require an additional 'inactField' entry to specify the boundary condition before bursting. The basic burst patch can be created with any mesh generation technique, but cyclic variations require the createBaffles utility.

**Content:**
The ability to have a "bursting" patch can be used with the burst (external patch), burstCyclic (internal with no AMR), or burstCyclicAMI (general internal). All fields require an additional entry in their dictionaries name ”inactField” which is used to specify the boundary condition of the field before bursting. The basic burst patch can be created using any mesh generation technique, however the cyclic variations require using the createBaffles utility, standard to OpenFOAM. See Sec. 20.5 for an example of how to create and use the patches.


---
## Section 16.1: setRefinedFields

**Summary:** This section describes 'setRefinedFields', a modified version of the standard OpenFOAM 'setFields' utility. It adds the capability to refine the mesh based on the fields being set, which is particularly useful for initializing small volumes within large computational cells. The utility allows for setting and refining 2D and 3D geometries, with refinement options specified in 'setFieldsDict'. It operates by setting default field values, applying the requested field modifications, and then checking for local variations to trigger refinement. The utility iterates until the mesh is stable or a maximum number of iterations is reached.

**Content:**
This is a modified version of the setFields utility in the standard Open-FOAM that includes the ability to refine the mesh based on the fields that are set. This is extremely beneficial when a small volume needs to be set within a set of very large computational cells. The same functionality presented in Sec. 11 is present, allowing for 2D and 3D geometries to be set and refined, and the refinement options are specified in setFieldsDict, not dynamicMeshDict. The utility works by setting the default field values provided, setting the requested fields, then checking for local variations in specific fields. Region may have a level defined where the mesh is refined up to this level. The default field values for any fields being set must also be defined so that the region is updated at each iteration. The utility will iterate until the mesh is no longer changing, or the maximum number of iterations is reached. By default, no error estimator will be used so any local change in a tracked field will be refined. However, by specifying an error estimator, the refinement will be determined by the method requested.
By default only fields that are specified within setFieldsDict will be modified, meaning that all other fields must use uniform field values to be read. The ’-updateAll’ command line option will read in all fields present in the current time step and update the sizes of the fields which is necessary if non-uniform fields will be used. If balancing is used this will automatically be enabled to ensure that all fields are correct. In addition to the standard shapes that can be set with setFields, several additional options have been added and are listed below with their required entries. All *MassToCell options require ”rho” and ”mass” to be specified.
Usage: setRefinedFields [OPTIONS]
New cell sets:
- sphereListToCell: centres, radii, or radius
- cylinderCellToList: p1s, p2s, radii, or radius
- boxMassToCell: centre and aspectRatio (span for backup)
- cylindericalMassToCell: centre, LbyD and direction (radius and L for backup)
- sphericalMassToCell: centre (radius for backup)
- hemisphericalMassToCell: centre (radius for backup)
- searchableSurfaceToCell: surface (searchable surface type)
- searchableSurfaceToPoint: surface (searchable surface type)
The option to specify non-uniform values for the fields has also been included and are specified by using the field type (i.e. volScalarField, pointVectorField, etc.) followed by the type (i.e. Value, Density, etc.), for example pointScalarFieldValue, volVectorFieldDensity, etc.
Field set types:
- Density: A density is calculated using the given volume integrated quantity, and dividing by the volume of the selected cells
- Function: Values are set based on the specified function.
- InitialValue: The current field is read and saved to ensure the true values are used after refinement.
- MassIntegrated: The mass integrated quantity read and is divided by the mass (density times volume) of the given cell set to determine the field value.
- Sum: The field value is calculated given a summed value and the list of fields to use in the sum
- Value: A uniform value is set


---
## Section 15.3: writeTimeList

**Summary:** This utility allows for writing output fields at a user-specified list of times, which is useful when data is needed at times that do not align with the standard write interval.

**Content:**
Additional write times are added using a list of user specified times. This is useful when fields are needed at time that do not correspond to a standard write interval. See validation/blastFoam/woodwardColella for an example.

**Parameters Table:**
[
  {
    "Variable": "times",
    "Description": "List of times to write"
  }
]


---
## Section 16.7: rotateFields

**Summary:** The 'rotateFields' utility maps fields from a 1D or 2D solution to a 2D or 3D solution, using the source and target meshes to determine the necessary rotation. It correctly maps vector and tensor fields by applying the local rotation tensor. Only fields present in the target case are mapped by default, but additional fields can be included. For cells outside the mapped region, the closest value from the source mesh is used. The utility also supports iterative refinement based on criteria in 'dynamicMeshDict' to facilitate initialization with AMR. It is noted that the current mapping is not conservative and uses nearest-cell values, which can lead to over- or under-emphasis of flow features.

**Content:**
The rotateFields utility is can be used to map the fields of a 1-D or 2-D solution to that of a 2-D or 3-D solution, where the source and target meshes are used to determine the rotation. The rotation of vector and tensor fields are mapped using the local rotation tensor to ensure that the correct radial components are used. Only field present in the target case are mapped, however additional fields can be mapped. Because there are no values for these fields, the closest value on the source mesh is used for cells outside of the mapped cells. Iterative refinement can be used to iteratively rotate fields and refine the mesh using the refinement criteria found in dynamicMeshDict allowing for easier initialization when using AMR.
NOTE: The mapping is not conservative currently so only the nearest cell value will be used which can result in certain flow features either being over or under emphasised.
Usage: rotateFields <sourceCase> [OPTIONS]

**Parameters Table:**
[
  {
    "Variable": "-sourceTime",
    "Description": "Time in the source case to map (default is startFrom time in source case)"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "-sourceRegion",
    "Description": "Region of the source case to map (only used for multi-region)"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "-targetRegion",
    "Description": "Region of the target case to map to (only used for multi-region)"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "-extend",
    "Description": "If a cell is outside of the source range, map to the closest cell (default is no)"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "-maxR",
    "Description": "Cutoff radius to map (default is a big number)"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "-centre",
    "Description": "Center of rotation on the source mesh (default is the center of the source mesh)"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "-uniform",
    "Description": "Copy uniform fields to the target case (default is no)"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "-additionalFields",
    "Description": "List of additional fields to map"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "-refine",
    "Description": "Iteratively map, rotate and refine the mesh"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "-parallelSource",
    "Description": "Map from a parallel source"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "-tets",
    "Description": "Use cell tet decomposition (no by default)"
  }
]


---
## Section 7.1.1: pressure

**Summary:** In this model, only pressure is used to determine if a burst occurs.

**Content:**
Only pressure is used to determine if burst occurs.

**Parameters Table:**
[
  {
    "Variable": "pName",
    "Description": "Name of the pressure field (p by default)"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "pRef",
    "Description": "Reference pressure (0 by default)"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "pBurst",
    "Description": "Pressure at which burst occurs [Pa]"
  }
]


---
## Section 7.1.2: impulse

**Summary:** In this model, only impulse is used to determine if a burst occurs. Since an impulse field must be available during the simulation, it is recommended to use the impulse functionObject.

**Content:**
Only impulse is used to determine if burst occurs. Because an impulse field must be available while running the simulation, it is recommended to use the impulse functionObject (Sec. 15), otherwise some other method of calculating impulse needs to be used.

**Parameters Table:**
[
  {
    "Variable": "impulseName",
    "Description": "Name of the impulse field (impulse by default)"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "impulseBurst",
    "Description": "Impulse at which burst occurs"
  }
]


---
## Section 7.1.3: pressureAndImpulse

**Summary:** This model uses a combination of pressure and impulse. A burst occurs if either model's condition is met. Both sets of inputs are required.

**Content:**
A combination of the pressure and impulse models are used. If either model results in a burst, then burst occurs. Both sets of inputs are required.


---
## Section 12.1.4: 2D lookup table

**Summary:** This section describes 2D lookup tables where data is retrieved based on 'x' and 'y' values. Data can be read from a table or a list, with a comma as the default delimiter. The table can be flipped (transposed) during reading if the input is specified as y-by-x. The implementation includes first and second derivatives (both pure and mixed) and supports reverse lookup for scalar data (e.g., finding 'x' given 'f' and 'y').

**Content:**
Data is looked up given x and y values. The data can be read from a table or from a list. The default delimiter is ",". The table can also be flipped when reading if the input is reversed (y by x) and a check is done on the size when reading. First and second derivatives (pure and mixed) have been implemented and a reverse lookup can be used for scalar data (find x or y given f and y or x).

## Section 8.1: Boundary conditions

**Summary:** This section describes several boundary conditions that have been implemented to make use of global patches.

**Content:**
Several boundary conditions have been implemented that make use of the global patches and are described below.


---
## Section 8.1.1: globalMapped

**Summary:** The globalMapped boundary condition can be used for both volume and point fields, directly mapping values from one patch to another, with the neighbor patch specified in regionProperties.

**Content:**
Both vol and point field can use the globalMapped boundary condition which directly maps values from one patch to another where the neighbour patch is specified in regionProperties.

**Parameters Table:**
[
  {
    "Variable": "nbrName",
    "Description": "Name of field to map from"
  }
]


---
## Section 8.1.2: globalInterpolated

**Summary:** The globalInterpolated boundary condition for point fields maps a volume field to a point field, with the neighbor patch specified in regionProperties.

**Content:**
Point field can use the globalInterpolated boundary condition which maps a volume field to a point field where the neighbour patch is specified in regionProperties.

**Parameters Table:**
[
  {
    "Variable": "nbrName",
    "Description": "Name of field to map from"
  }
]


---
## Section 8: Region models

**Summary:** This chapter explains that when multiple regions are used, blastFoam solvers use global poly patches to transfer data between fields without requiring special mesh patches. The information is transferred using field-specific boundary conditions, with the interpolation method, sampled patch, and optional displacement field specified in the constant/regionProperties file.

**Content:**
When multiple regions are used, blastFoam solvers use global poly patches which do not require any special mesh patches to transfer data between field. Instead, the information is transferred using field specific boundary conditions. The interpolation method, sampled patch, and optional displacement field is specified in the constant/regionProperties file. An example of regionProperties can be seen below


---
## Section 8.1.3: globalTemperatureCoupled

**Summary:** Using the same methodology as the globalMapped boundary condition, the globalTemperatureCoupled boundary condition computes the temperature of a patch using the neighbor temperature, heat transfer coefficient, and radiation heat flux. An unmapped temperature can be specified for cases where the entire boundary does not overlap the neighbor patch.

**Content:**
Using the same methodology as the globalMapped boundary condition, the globalTemperatureCoupled boundary condition computes the temperature of a patch using the neighbour temperature, heat transfer coefficient, and radiation heat flux. Optionally when the entire boundary does not overlap the neighbour patch and unmapped temperature can be specified.

**Parameters Table:**
[
  {
    "Variable": "TNbr",
    "Description": "Name of neighbour temperature (T by default)"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "hNbr",
    "Description": "Name of neighbour convective heat transfer coefficient (none by default)"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "h",
    "Description": "Name of convective heat transfer coefficient (none by default)"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "qrNbr",
    "Description": "Name of neighbour radiation heat flux (none by default)"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "qr",
    "Description": "Name of radiation heat flux (T by default)"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "limitGrad",
    "Description": "Is the temperature gradient limited (no by default)"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "unmappedT",
    "Description": "Unmapped temperature (293.15 by default)"
  }
]


---
## Section 19.6: blastMultiRegionFoam

**Summary:** The 'blastMultiRegionFoam' solver is derived from the 'chtMultiRegionFoam' solver in OpenFOAM. The main differences are the use of ODE time integration and Riemann fluxes. Thermal transport in solids is still handled implicitly using a matrix solution.

**Content:**
The blastMultiRegionFoam solver is derived from the chtMultiRegion-Foam available in OpenFOAM. The major differences are in the use of ODE time integration and Riemann fluxes. The thermal transport in solids is still treated implicitly through a matrix solution.


---
## Section 11.2: Dynamic Load Balancing

**Summary:** This section discusses the availability of dynamic load balancing for 2-D, 2-D axisymmetric, and 3-D meshes, which can significantly reduce computational time by distributing cells evenly across processors. However, it also highlights known limitations. Large cases may exceed the MPI buffer limit during data transfer, causing crashes. Additionally, issues can arise when transferring non-scalar fields and with 'movingWall' boundary conditions. While these problems have been addressed for the standard 'blastFoam' solver, stability is not guaranteed if 'adaptiveFvMesh' is used with a different solver.

**Content:**
The ability to use dynamic load balancing is available for 2-D, 2-D axisymmetric, and 3-D meshes. Dynamic load balancing can greatly reduce the computational time required by balancing the cells equally across all processors. However, there are known limitations: due to the way OpenFOAM transfers data between processors, the MPI buffer limit can be exceeded for large cases, leading to the simulation crashing. There are also known problems in the transferring of data for calculated non-scalar fields and movingWall boundary conditions. The fields used within the standard blastFoam solver have addressed this, but if adaptiveFvMesh is used with a different solver, its stability with balancing is not guaranteed.

**Parameters Table:**
[
  {
    "Variable": "errorEstimators",
    "Description": "List of error estimators to be used"
  }
]


---
## Section 19.3: blastFSIFoam

**Summary:** The 'blastFSIFoam' solver handles fluid-solid interaction by using the standard 'blastFoam' solver for the fluid phase and models from Section 13 for the solid deformation. The fluid phase employs a moving mesh, while the solid phase can be solved using a total Lagrangian, linear (stationary), or updated Lagrangian (moving) method. Information transfer between patches is managed by the 'coupledGlobalPolyPatch' from Cardiff et al. (2018), which does not require changes to mesh patch types. Fields needing coupling information (like temperature or displacement) should use a global patch type (see Section 8.1). If meshes are created for each region individually, patch types can be set as usual; however, if the 'splitMeshRegions' utility is used, patch names and types may need modification. This solver is still in development.

**Content:**
The blastFSIFoam solver uses the standard blastFoam solver to solve the fluid phase, and the models mentioned in Sec. 13 to handle the solid deformation. The fluid phase uses a moving mesh, while the solid phase can be solved using a total lagrangian or linear (stationary), or updated Lagrangian (moving) method. In order to correctly transfer the information between the patches, the coupledGlobalPolyPatch Cardiff et al. (2018) is used and does not require any changed to the mesh patch types. Fields that require coupling information (temperature, displacment, etc.) should used a global patch type (see Sec. 8.1). If meshes are made for each individual region, the patch types can be specified as usual. However, if the splitMeshRegions utility is used to separate a single region mesh into multiple region meshes, the patch names and types may need to be modified
NOTE: This solver is still in development. Please use with caution.


---
## Section 5.9: Initialization

**Summary:** This section describes the initialization of fields, which can be done in several ways, though density, pressure, and temperature must be given as initial conditions. For Mie-Grüneisen equations of state, pressure and density are used by default to initialize internal energy. For temperature-based equations, density and temperature are used. If the 'calculateDensity' keyword is set to 'yes', density is calculated from pressure and temperature before internal energy initialization. If the internal energy field is provided in the initial conditions, pressure and temperature are calculated directly from it and the density.

**Content:**
The initialization of fields can be handled in several ways, but ρi, p, and T must be given as initial conditions. Some of these fields may be overwritten, but the boundary conditions are used to construct the conserved quantities. For equations of state in the Mie-Grüneisen form, by default the pressure and density are used to initialize the internal energy. Temperature based equations of state initialize the internal energy using the density and temperature. If the ”calculateDensity” keyword is defined, and is set to ”yes” in the given equation of state dictionary, then the density is calculated from the pressure and temperature prior to the initialization of the internal energy. Each of the initialization methods use a Newton-Raphson root finding method to find the necessary quantities. If the internal energy field is provided in the initial conditions folder, the pressure and temperature fields are calculated directly from the density and internal energy.


---
## Section 7.1: Burst models

**Summary:** This section explains that the method for calculating when a burst occurs is runtime-selectable, with inputs placed in the constant/polyMesh/boundary file. The settings can also be added within the patch entry of blockMeshDict or snappyHexMeshDict.

**Content:**
The method to calculate where burst occurs is runtime-selectable and the inputs are placed in the constant/polyMesh/boundary file under the corresponding boundary. This can also be added in either the patch entry within blockMeshDict of snappyHexMeshDict.

**Parameters Table:**
[
  {
    "Variable": "partialBurst",
    "Description": "Can individual face burst (no by default)"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "useDelta",
    "Description": "Us the difference across coupled patches rather than the maximum value (yes by default)"
  }
]

## Section 14: Coupling to preCICE’s OpenFOAM adapter

**Summary:** This chapter explains the capability of coupling blastFoam with an external solver through the preCICE library. This feature is enabled by the addition of moving mesh functionality and is ready to use upon installation without special setup for standard moving mesh classes. However, when using the 'movingAdaptiveFvMesh', there are limitations: the coupled boundary cannot change during the simulation, which means load balancing is not allowed, and the 'protectedPatches' keyword must be used to specify boundaries that cannot be refined. Additionally, checkpointing cannot be used with refinement due to mesh-related limitations.

**Content:**
With the addition of moving meshes, the ability to couple blastFoam to an external solver through preCICE is possible. There is no special setup required, and it is ready to be used upon install. There are no limitations for the standard blastFoam moving mesh classes. However, when using the movingAdaptiveFvMesh, the coupled boundary cannot change within the simulation. For this reason, balancing is not allowed and the optional ”protectedPatches” keyword must be used to specify boundaries which cannot be refined. Checkpointing can also not be used with refinement due to limitations on the mesh.


---
## Section 8.1: Boundary conditions

**Summary:** This section describes several boundary conditions that have been implemented to make use of global patches.

**Content:**
Several boundary conditions have been implemented that make use of the global patches and are described below.


---
## Section 8: Region models

**Summary:** This chapter explains that when multiple regions are used, blastFoam solvers use global poly patches to transfer data between fields without requiring special mesh patches. The information is transferred using field-specific boundary conditions, with the interpolation method, sampled patch, and optional displacement field specified in the constant/regionProperties file.

**Content:**
When multiple regions are used, blastFoam solvers use global poly patches which do not require any special mesh patches to transfer data between field. Instead, the information is transferred using field specific boundary conditions. The interpolation method, sampled patch, and optional displacement field is specified in the constant/regionProperties file. An example of regionProperties can be seen below


---
## Section 7: Burst patches

**Summary:** This chapter describes the use of 'bursting' patches, including burst (external), burstCyclic (internal without AMR), and burstCyclicAMI (general internal). All fields for these patches require an additional 'inactField' entry to specify the boundary condition before bursting. The basic burst patch can be created with any mesh generation technique, but cyclic variations require the createBaffles utility.

**Content:**
The ability to have a "bursting" patch can be used with the burst (external patch), burstCyclic (internal with no AMR), or burstCyclicAMI (general internal). All fields require an additional entry in their dictionaries name ”inactField” which is used to specify the boundary condition of the field before bursting. The basic burst patch can be created using any mesh generation technique, however the cyclic variations require using the createBaffles utility, standard to OpenFOAM. See Sec. 20.5 for an example of how to create and use the patches.


---
## Section 7.1: Burst models

**Summary:** This section explains that the method for calculating when a burst occurs is runtime-selectable, with inputs placed in the constant/polyMesh/boundary file. The settings can also be added within the patch entry of blockMeshDict or snappyHexMeshDict.

**Content:**
The method to calculate where burst occurs is runtime-selectable and the inputs are placed in the constant/polyMesh/boundary file under the corresponding boundary. This can also be added in either the patch entry within blockMeshDict of snappyHexMeshDict.

**Parameters Table:**
[
  {
    "Variable": "partialBurst",
    "Description": "Can individual face burst (no by default)"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "useDelta",
    "Description": "Us the difference across coupled patches rather than the maximum value (yes by default)"
  }
]

## Section 5.3.3: logPolynomial

**Summary:** This transport package uses polynomial functions of the natural logarithm of temperature for the natural logarithm of dynamic viscosity and thermal conductivity.

**Content:**
Transport package using polynomial functions of the natural logarithm of temperature for the natural logarithm of dynamic viscosity and thermal conductivity:

$$\log(\mu(T)) = \sum_{i=0}^{7} \mu_i(\log(T))^i \quad (5.78)$$

$$\log(\kappa(T)) = \sum_{i=0}^{7} \kappa_i(\log(T))^i \quad (5.79)$$

**Parameters Table:**
[
  {
    "Variable": "muCoeffs<8>",
    "Description": "Dynamic viscosity coefficients"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "kappaCoeffs<8>",
    "Description": "Conductivity coefficients"
  }
]

detailed_results.query: Where is the reacting diameter model documented?
detailed_results.raw_results:
--- Retrieved Documentation Information ---

## Section 9.3: reacting

**Summary:** The reacting diameter model adds a source term to the diameter transport equation, causing the local diameter to increase or decrease based on a given reaction rate.

**Content:**
The reacting diameter model adds an additional source term in the transport equation for the diameter to increase/decrease the local diameter based on the given reaction rate

$$\frac{\partial d_i}{\partial t} + \mathbf{u}_i \cdot \nabla d_i = k \quad (9.3)$$

where k is the specified reaction rate (see Sec. 9.4)

**Parameters Table:**
[
  {
    "Variable": "d",
    "Description": "Initial diameter [m]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "reactionRate",
    "Description": "Reaction rate model"
  }
]


---
## Section 3.5.2: reactingParticle

**Summary:** In this model, the mass transfer rate is explicitly calculated from the change in a particle's mass. The regression rate is determined by a reaction rate, requiring the use of the reactingParticle diameter model.

**Content:**
The mass transfer rate is explicitly calculated using the change in mass of a particle where the regression rate is determined by a reaction rate. the reactingParticle diameter model must be used.


---
## Section 19.5: blastReactingFoam

**Summary:** The 'blastReactingFoam' solver uses standard OpenFOAM thermodynamic classes to solve multi-species systems with reactions, including combustion models. It requires the 'constant/thermophysicalProperties', 'constant/combustionProperties', and 'constant/chemistryProperties' dictionaries, along with initial mass fraction files. The conserved quantities (mass, momentum, energy) and mass fractions are all transported using the flux schemes described in this guide.

**Content:**
blastReactingFoam uses the standard OpenFOAM thermodynamic classes, and can be used to solve multi-specie systems that include reactions. The use of combustion models are also available. The constant/thermophysicalProperties dictionary must be included under the constant dictionary along with the relevant dictionaries (constant/combustionProperties and constant/chemistyProperties) and initial value files (mass fractions). The conserved quantities (mass, momentum, and energy) as well as the mass fractions are all transported using the flux schemes presented herein.


---
## Section 9: Diameter models

**Summary:** This chapter describes the models available for the diameter of a dispersed phase, which is used to calculate surface reaction rates in the Arrhenius activation model and interfacial transfer sources in the blastEulerFoam solver.

**Content:**
The diameter of a dispersed phase is used to calculate surface reaction rates in the Arrhenius activation model as well as interfacial transfer sources in the blastEulerFoam solver. Below are is a short description of the models currently available.


---
## Section 9.1: constant

**Summary:** This model uses a constant diameter, and the diameter field is not written.

**Content:**
A constant diameter is used.

NOTE: The diameter field is not written

**Parameters Table:**
[
  {
    "Variable": "d",
    "Description": "Diameter of the phase [m]"
  }
]


---
## Section 9.2: constantMass

**Summary:** In this model, the diameter is determined using a reference diameter at a reference density. The mass is defined, and the true diameter is calculated from it.

**Content:**
The diameter is determined using a reference diameter at a reference density. The mass is defined as

$$M = \frac{\pi d_0^3 \rho_0}{6} \quad (9.1)$$

and the true diameter is calculated using

$$d = \left(\frac{6M}{\rho\pi}\right)^{1/3} \quad (9.2)$$

**Parameters Table:**
[
  {
    "Variable": "rho0",
    "Description": "Reference density [kg/m3]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "d0",
    "Description": "Reference diameter [m]"
  }
]


---
## Section 5.6.1.5: ArrheniusRate

**Summary:** A more physical representation of the activation rate that uses the local temperature, which is defined by the internal energy and specific heat of the mixture. The reaction progress variable's evolution equation is given, with the particle diameter determined by a chosen diameter model.

**Content:**
A more physical representation of the activation rate uses the local temperature, defined by the internal energy and the specific heat of the mixture. The evolution equation for the reaction progress variable is

$$\dot{R}_i = (1 - \lambda) \begin{cases} 0 & T < T_{ign} \\ d_p^2 A_{low} \exp(\frac{E_{a,low}}{RT}) & T_{ign} \le T < T_s \\ A_{high} \exp(\frac{E_{a,high}}{RT}) & T \ge T_s \end{cases} \quad (5.94)$$

Where the particle diameter is determined by the chosen diameter model (see Sec. 9), and R is the ideal gas constant. This model is still in development and has not been validated

**Parameters Table:**
[
  {
    "Variable": "diameterModel",
    "Description": "Diameter model used to determine the particle diameter"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "ALow",
    "Description": "Low activation rate [m\u22122 s\u22121]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "EaLow",
    "Description": "Low activation energy [m2/s2]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "AHigh",
    "Description": "High activation rate [s\u22121]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "EaHigh",
    "Description": "High activation energy [m2/s2]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "Ts",
    "Description": "Switch temperature [K]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "Tign",
    "Description": "Minimum temperature of reaction"
  }
]


---
## Section 4.6.2: YuStandish

**Summary:** The Yu-Standish packing model (Yu and Standish, 1987) assumes that particle phases are ordered from smallest to largest diameter to calculate the packing limit.

**Content:**
The Yu Standish packing model (Yu and Standish, 1987) assumes that the particle phases are ordered from smallest diameter to largest for the calculation of the packing limit. The packing limit is defined as

$$\alpha_{max,s} = \min_{i \in 1:N} \left[ \frac{\alpha_{max,i}}{1 - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} (1 - \frac{\alpha_{max,i}}{p_{ik}}) \frac{cx_i}{X_{ik}} - \sum_{k=i+1}^{N} (1 - \frac{\alpha_{max,i}}{p_{ik}}) \frac{cx_i}{X_{ik}}} \right] \quad (4.40)$$

where

$$cx_i = \frac{\alpha_i}{\alpha_s} \quad (4.41)$$

$$X_{ik} = \begin{cases} \frac{1-r_{ik}^2}{2-\alpha_{max,i}} & \text{if } k < i \\ 1 - \frac{1-r_{ik}^2}{2-\alpha_{max,i}} & \text{if } k \ge i \end{cases} \quad (4.42)$$

$$p_{ik} = \begin{cases} \alpha_{max,i} + \alpha_{max,i}(1 - \alpha_{max,i}) \times (1 - 2.35r_{ik} + 1.35r_{ik}^2) & \text{if } r_{ik} \le 0.741 \\ \alpha_{max,i} & \text{if } r_{ik} > 0.741 \end{cases} \quad (4.43)$$

and

$$r_{ik} = \begin{cases} \frac{d_i}{d_k} & \text{if } i \ge k \\ \frac{d_k}{d_i} & \text{if } i < k \end{cases} \quad (4.44)$$

**Parameters Table:**
[
  {
    "Variable": "residualAlpha",
    "Description": "Minimum volume fraction to divide by [ ]"
  }
]


---
## Section 5.5: Basic thermodynamic model

**Summary:** This section explains that the basicFluid and basicSolid models ('type' basic) use a transport model, a thermodynamic model, and an equation of state to describe a non-reacting phase.

**Content:**
The basicFluid and basicSolid models (”type” basic) uses a transport model, thermodynamic model, and equation of state to describe a phase. The basic model should be used for non-reacting phases.


---
## Section 9.4.1: pressureBased

**Summary:** The pressureBased reaction rate model computes the rate using a coefficient, pressure scale, pressure offset (in scaled units), and pressure exponent.

**Content:**
The pressureBased reaction rate uses a coefficient, pressure scale, pressure offset (in scaled units) and pressure exponent to compute the rate

$$k = \beta(\bar{p})^{\alpha} \quad (9.4)$$

and p¯ = p × pscale. For example, if the pressure relation is in MPa, pscale = 1e − 6.

**Parameters Table:**
[
  {
    "Variable": "alpha",
    "Description": "Pressure exponent [ ]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "beta",
    "Description": "Coefficient [Pa\u2212\u03b1]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "pScale",
    "Description": "Pressure scale [ ]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "offset",
    "Description": "Pressure offset [Pa/pScale]"
  }
]

detailed_results.query: Which section groups the reaction-rate models used by the diameter model?

--- Retrieved Documentation Information ---

## Section 9.4: Reaction Rates

**Summary:** This section explains that reaction rates are used to give rates based on temperature and/or pressure. There are currently two options: pressure-based and Arrhenius rate.

**Content:**
Reaction rates are used to give rates based on temperature and/or pressure currently there are two options, pressure based, and Arrhenius rate


---
## Section 9: Diameter models

**Summary:** This chapter describes the models available for the diameter of a dispersed phase, which is used to calculate surface reaction rates in the Arrhenius activation model and interfacial transfer sources in the blastEulerFoam solver.

**Content:**
The diameter of a dispersed phase is used to calculate surface reaction rates in the Arrhenius activation model as well as interfacial transfer sources in the blastEulerFoam solver. Below are is a short description of the models currently available.


---
## Section 5.6.1.5: ArrheniusRate

**Summary:** A more physical representation of the activation rate that uses the local temperature, which is defined by the internal energy and specific heat of the mixture. The reaction progress variable's evolution equation is given, with the particle diameter determined by a chosen diameter model.

**Content:**
A more physical representation of the activation rate uses the local temperature, defined by the internal energy and the specific heat of the mixture. The evolution equation for the reaction progress variable is

$$\dot{R}_i = (1 - \lambda) \begin{cases} 0 & T < T_{ign} \\ d_p^2 A_{low} \exp(\frac{E_{a,low}}{RT}) & T_{ign} \le T < T_s \\ A_{high} \exp(\frac{E_{a,high}}{RT}) & T \ge T_s \end{cases} \quad (5.94)$$

Where the particle diameter is determined by the chosen diameter model (see Sec. 9), and R is the ideal gas constant. This model is still in development and has not been validated

**Parameters Table:**
[
  {
    "Variable": "diameterModel",
    "Description": "Diameter model used to determine the particle diameter"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "ALow",
    "Description": "Low activation rate [m\u22122 s\u22121]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "EaLow",
    "Description": "Low activation energy [m2/s2]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "AHigh",
    "Description": "High activation rate [s\u22121]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "EaHigh",
    "Description": "High activation energy [m2/s2]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "Ts",
    "Description": "Switch temperature [K]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "Tign",
    "Description": "Minimum temperature of reaction"
  }
]


---
## Section 9.3: reacting

**Summary:** The reacting diameter model adds a source term to the diameter transport equation, causing the local diameter to increase or decrease based on a given reaction rate.

**Content:**
The reacting diameter model adds an additional source term in the transport equation for the diameter to increase/decrease the local diameter based on the given reaction rate

$$\frac{\partial d_i}{\partial t} + \mathbf{u}_i \cdot \nabla d_i = k \quad (9.3)$$

where k is the specified reaction rate (see Sec. 9.4)

**Parameters Table:**
[
  {
    "Variable": "d",
    "Description": "Initial diameter [m]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "reactionRate",
    "Description": "Reaction rate model"
  }
]


---
## Section 9.4.1: pressureBased

**Summary:** The pressureBased reaction rate model computes the rate using a coefficient, pressure scale, pressure offset (in scaled units), and pressure exponent.

**Content:**
The pressureBased reaction rate uses a coefficient, pressure scale, pressure offset (in scaled units) and pressure exponent to compute the rate

$$k = \beta(\bar{p})^{\alpha} \quad (9.4)$$

and p¯ = p × pscale. For example, if the pressure relation is in MPa, pscale = 1e − 6.

**Parameters Table:**
[
  {
    "Variable": "alpha",
    "Description": "Pressure exponent [ ]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "beta",
    "Description": "Coefficient [Pa\u2212\u03b1]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "pScale",
    "Description": "Pressure scale [ ]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "offset",
    "Description": "Pressure offset [Pa/pScale]"
  }
]


---
## Section 9.4.2: Arrhenius

**Summary:** This model gives the reaction rate based on the Arrhenius reaction rate formula.

**Content:**
The rate given by the Arrhenius reaction rate is

$$k = AT^{\beta}\exp\left(-\frac{T_a}{T}\right) \quad (9.5)$$

**Parameters Table:**
[
  {
    "Variable": "A",
    "Description": "Rate constant [1/s]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "beta",
    "Description": "Temperature exponent [ ]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "Ta",
    "Description": "Activation temperature [K]"
  }
]


---
## Section 3.5.2: reactingParticle

**Summary:** In this model, the mass transfer rate is explicitly calculated from the change in a particle's mass. The regression rate is determined by a reaction rate, requiring the use of the reactingParticle diameter model.

**Content:**
The mass transfer rate is explicitly calculated using the change in mass of a particle where the regression rate is determined by a reaction rate. the reactingParticle diameter model must be used.


---
## Section 5.6.1: Activation Models

**Summary:** This section describes different models available to approximate the speed at which solid reactants become product gases, including linear, pressure-based, and Arrhenius rate reaction models. These models use a reaction progress variable to describe the state of cell activation. To ensure numerical stability, the initial energy is defined by the unreacted equation of state, and reaction energy is added based on the rate of change of the reaction progress variable. Phase properties are calculated by blending the unreacted and reacted states. Detonation points can be set for any activation model to specify when and where cells are fully activated. The transport of the reaction progress variable is also described.

**Content:**
In order to accurately model the release of energy, different models can be used to approximate the speed at which the solid reactants become the product gases. The currently available models include the linear model, a pressure based model, and finally an Arrhenius rate reaction. Theses models all use a reaction progress variable, λi, to describe the state of individual cell activation. Additionally, in order to add numerical stability, the initial energy is defined using the unreacted equation of state, and the reaction energy, E0, is added based on the time rate of change of the reaction progress variable, i.e.

$$\dot{E} = \frac{E_0\rho_i}{\rho_{0,i}}\frac{d\lambda}{dt} \quad (5.86)$$

The phase properties are calculated using

$$x_i = \lambda_i^l x_{r,i} + (1 - \lambda_i^l)x_{u,i}) \quad (5.87)$$

where xr,i is the reacted phase quantity, xu,i is the unreacted phase quantity, and l is the exponent used to blend the unreacted and reacted states (lambdaPow). By default l is one. The advection scheme and limiters for λi need to be specified in the fvSchemes in the same manner as the other transported quantities. The setting of detonation points can be done for any activation model. By setting detonation points, the cells within the charge with the radii specified are set to full activated when run time reaches the given delays. If no radii are provided, only the cell containing the point will be activated. Additionally, delays can be used to specify the time at which the given point and cells within the radius are activated. For a single detonation point, the ”useCOM” option can be used to calculate the center of mass of an individual charge. Transport of the reaction progress variable is described by the transport equation

$$\frac{\partial \alpha_i\rho_i\lambda_i}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\alpha_i\rho_i \mathbf{u} \lambda_i) = \alpha_i\rho_i \dot{R}_i \quad (5.88)$$

where Ri is the reaction rate determined by the model of choice.

**Parameters Table:**
[
  {
    "Variable": "E0",
    "Description": "Detonation energy [Pa]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "points",
    "Description": "Detonation points [m]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "radii (radius)",
    "Description": "Initiation radii around detonation points (default is 0) [m]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "delays",
    "Description": "Delay time of detonation points (default is 0) [s]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "lambdaPow",
    "Description": "Exponent used for blending [ ] (default is 1)"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "useCOM",
    "Description": "Is the center of mass used as the detation point (default is no)"
  }
]


---
## Section 4.7: Granular drag models

**Summary:** This section explains that the drag force between granular phases differs from standard drag models because collisions, which are a function of granular energy, significantly affect the rate at which velocities relax. These models are only necessary when multiple granular phases are used.

**Content:**
The drag force between granular phases is different than standard drags models due to the fact the the collisions which are a function of the granular energy strongly effect the rate at which the velocities relax. These granular drag models are only necessary when multiple granular phases are used.


---
## Section 9.1: constant

**Summary:** This model uses a constant diameter, and the diameter field is not written.

**Content:**
A constant diameter is used.

NOTE: The diameter field is not written

**Parameters Table:**
[
  {
    "Variable": "d",
    "Description": "Diameter of the phase [m]"
  }
]

Which section introduces the available Riemann solvers in BlastFoam?
detailed_results.raw_results:
--- Retrieved Documentation Information ---

## Section 10.1: Riemann Solvers

**Summary:** This section describes the available Riemann solvers. For all schemes, n denotes the surface normal vector, and u represents the normal velocity component. The solutions include the volume fraction for multi-fluid problems, but these details are omitted from the descriptions, except for the AUSM+up flux scheme for granular phases.

**Content:**
The currently available Riemann solvers are described below. For all schemes, n denotes the surface normal vector, and u = u · n. There is the inclusion of volume fraction in the solution of multi-fluid problems, however with the exception of the AUSM+up flux scheme for granular phases these changes will not be included in the descriptions below.


---
## Section 19.6: blastMultiRegionFoam

**Summary:** The 'blastMultiRegionFoam' solver is derived from the 'chtMultiRegionFoam' solver in OpenFOAM. The main differences are the use of ODE time integration and Riemann fluxes. Thermal transport in solids is still handled implicitly using a matrix solution.

**Content:**
The blastMultiRegionFoam solver is derived from the chtMultiRegion-Foam available in OpenFOAM. The major differences are in the use of ODE time integration and Riemann fluxes. The thermal transport in solids is still treated implicitly through a matrix solution.


---
## Section 19.7: blastParcelFoam

**Summary:** The 'blastParcelFoam' solver uses standard OpenFOAM Lagrangian classes to simulate a coupled system of a Eulerian fluid and a collection of Lagrangian parcels. It includes interaction terms for drag, heat transfer, and particle collisions. Not all standard OpenFOAM Lagrangian options are available due to the use of a different thermodynamic model. While AMR can be used with this solver, dynamic load balancing cannot.

**Content:**
blastParcelFoam makes use of the standard OpenFOAM Lagrangian classes to solve a coupled Eulerian fluid and collection of Lagragian parcels. This includes interaction terms like drag, heat transfer, and particle collisions. Not all of the OpenFOAM standard Lagrangian options are available due to the use of a different thermodynamic model. While AMR can be used, dynamic load balancing cannot.


---
## Section 19.1: blastFoam

**Summary:** The 'blastFoam' solver is the standard solver, capable of handling single-phase, two-phase, and multiphase flows. The phases and thermodynamic models are specified in the 'constant/phaseProperties' file. If a list with more than one phase is provided, the two-phase or multiphase solver is automatically selected; otherwise, the single-phase solver is used. All phases utilize the blastFoam-specific equations of state described in Section 5.

**Content:**
The blastFoam solver is the standard solver and has a variety of uses, including single phase, two phase and multiphase flows, where the phases and thermodynamic models are specified in the constant/phaseProperties. When a list of phases is provided (and the list has more than one entry), the two phase or multiphase solver is selected. If the key word phases is not specified, then the single phase solver is selected. All phases use the blastFoam specific equations of states (see Section 5).


---
## Section 19.5: blastReactingFoam

**Summary:** The 'blastReactingFoam' solver uses standard OpenFOAM thermodynamic classes to solve multi-species systems with reactions, including combustion models. It requires the 'constant/thermophysicalProperties', 'constant/combustionProperties', and 'constant/chemistryProperties' dictionaries, along with initial mass fraction files. The conserved quantities (mass, momentum, energy) and mass fractions are all transported using the flux schemes described in this guide.

**Content:**
blastReactingFoam uses the standard OpenFOAM thermodynamic classes, and can be used to solve multi-specie systems that include reactions. The use of combustion models are also available. The constant/thermophysicalProperties dictionary must be included under the constant dictionary along with the relevant dictionaries (constant/combustionProperties and constant/chemistyProperties) and initial value files (mass fractions). The conserved quantities (mass, momentum, and energy) as well as the mass fractions are all transported using the flux schemes presented herein.


---
## Section 19.2: blastEulerFoam

**Summary:** The 'blastEulerFoam' solver is an Euler-Euler (two/multifluid) variant of blastFoam, based on the work of Houim and Oran (2016) and Lai et al. (2018). It currently supports fluid-solid simulations, with fluid-fluid capabilities under development. A key advantage over the standard 'multiphaseEulerFoam' is its ability to handle any number of particle phases. It supports three phase models: 'fluid' (for single-component fluids like air), 'multiphase' (for fluids with multiple components, each with a unique equation of state), and 'granular' (for solid phases). Fluid and multiphase models can use any of the fluid thermodynamic models, while the granular model uses solid thermodynamic models. Interactions between fluids are described in Sections 3 and 4. A note clarifies that while multiple fluid phases are technically supported, simulations of that type are not currently the intended use.

**Content:**
The blastEulerFoam solver is the Euler-Euler (two/multifluid) variant of blastFoam. Currently only fluid-solid simulations are supported; however, the addition of fluid-fluid simulations is under development. This solver is based on the work of Houim and Oran (2016) and Lai et al. (2018). One major advantage over the standard multiphaseEulerFoam solver is that any number of particle phases can be used. Currently there are three phase models that can be used: fluid, multiphase, and granular. The fluid phase model is used to describe a single component fluid (such as air). The multiphase phase model is used to describe a fluid phase consisting of multiple phases, each described by a unique equation of state. This allows simulations of standard detonation problems with the addition of particles. The granular phase model is used to describe a solid phase. Both the fluid and multiphase models can use any of the fluid thermodynamic models, while the granular model can use any of the solid thermodynamic models. The models describing interactions between fluids are described in Sec. 3 and Sec. 4
NOTE: While multiple fluid phases will not cause the solver to fail, theses types of simulations are not currently supported.


---
## Section 19.3: blastFSIFoam

**Summary:** The 'blastFSIFoam' solver handles fluid-solid interaction by using the standard 'blastFoam' solver for the fluid phase and models from Section 13 for the solid deformation. The fluid phase employs a moving mesh, while the solid phase can be solved using a total Lagrangian, linear (stationary), or updated Lagrangian (moving) method. Information transfer between patches is managed by the 'coupledGlobalPolyPatch' from Cardiff et al. (2018), which does not require changes to mesh patch types. Fields needing coupling information (like temperature or displacement) should use a global patch type (see Section 8.1). If meshes are created for each region individually, patch types can be set as usual; however, if the 'splitMeshRegions' utility is used, patch names and types may need modification. This solver is still in development.

**Content:**
The blastFSIFoam solver uses the standard blastFoam solver to solve the fluid phase, and the models mentioned in Sec. 13 to handle the solid deformation. The fluid phase uses a moving mesh, while the solid phase can be solved using a total lagrangian or linear (stationary), or updated Lagrangian (moving) method. In order to correctly transfer the information between the patches, the coupledGlobalPolyPatch Cardiff et al. (2018) is used and does not require any changed to the mesh patch types. Fields that require coupling information (temperature, displacment, etc.) should used a global patch type (see Sec. 8.1). If meshes are made for each individual region, the patch types can be specified as usual. However, if the splitMeshRegions utility is used to separate a single region mesh into multiple region meshes, the patch names and types may need to be modified
NOTE: This solver is still in development. Please use with caution.


---
## Section 19: Solvers

**Summary:** This chapter provides a brief description of the wide variety of solvers available in blastFoam for simulating transient, highly compressible flows.

**Content:**
blastFoam offers a wide variety of solvers for simulating transient, highly compressible flows. A short description of each is given below.


---
## Section 19.4: blastXiFoam

**Summary:** This is an additional solver for combustion, extending the standard OpenFOAM 'XiFoam' solver. The key difference is the use of the flux schemes presented earlier to transport conserved quantities, which provides a more accurate description of highly compressible combustion phenomena like deflagration-to-detonation transition. It is recommended to use the 'adiabaticFlameT' utility to calculate model coefficients for the 'constant/thermophysicalProperties' dictionary.

**Content:**
In addition to the standard blastFoam solver, an additional solver has been added to solve combustion as an extension of the standard OpenFOAM Xi-Foam solver. The only difference is that the flux schemes presented earlier are used to transport the conserved quantities. This allows for a more accurate description of combusting, highly compressible flows (i.e. deflagration to detonation transition). It is recommended to use the adibaticFlameT utility to calculate the model coefficients used in the required constant/thermophysicalProperties dictionary.


---
## Section 15.8: blastMachNo

**Summary:** This function object replaces the standard 'machNo' utility in OpenFOAM. It calculates the Mach number using the speed of sound derived from the specific thermodynamic models available in blastFoam.

**Content:**
The blastMachNo replaces the standard machNo available in OpenFOAM by using the speed of sound calculated from the blastFoam specific thermodynamic models.

**Parameters Table:**
[
  {
    "Variable": "phaseName",
    "Description": "Name of the phase (default is none)"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "UName",
    "Description": "Name of the velocity field (default is U or U.phaseName)"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "result",
    "Description": "Name of the Mach number field (default is Ma or Ma.phaseName)"
  }
]

detailed_results.query: Where is the AUSM+up flux scheme with low-speed correction documented?
detailed_results.raw_results:
--- Retrieved Documentation Information ---

## Section 10.1.5: AUSM+up

**Summary:** The AUSM+up scheme extends the AUSM+ scheme with a low-speed correction by Liou (2006). It uses the high-speed portion from the previous section but adds diffusion terms, resulting in new definitions for the mass and pressure fluxes. This correction is important for blast simulations where Mach numbers can become much less than unity, which can lead to numerical checker-boarding.

**Content:**
The AUSM+ scheme extended to include a low speed correction by Liou (2006). The previous section is used to describe the high speed portion, however additional diffusion terms are added resulting in the following definitions of the mass and pressure fluxes

$$M_* = M^+_{4,own} + M^-_{4,nei} + M_p \quad (10.24)$$

and

$$P_* = P^+_{5,own}p_{own} + P^-_{5,nei}p_{nei} + P_u \quad (10.25)$$

where

$$M_p = -\frac{K_p}{f_a}\max(1 - \sigma \bar{M}a^2, 0) \frac{p_{nei} - p_{own}}{\rho_* c_*^2} \quad (10.26)$$

$$P_u = -K_u P^+_{5,own} P^-_{5,nei} (\rho_{own} + \rho_{nei}) f_a c_* (u_{nei} - u_{own}) \quad (10.27)$$

and fa = 1, σ = 1, Kp = 0.25, Ku = 0.75, and α is redefined as 3/16(5fa − 4). The interface density, ρ*, is defined as (ρown+ρnei)/2, and the average Mach number is defined as.

$$\bar{M}a^2 = \frac{u_{own}^2 + u_{nei}^2}{2c_*^2} \quad (10.28)$$

This correction is very important when considering blasts because the Mach numbers can become much less than unity, resulting in numerical checker-boarding.


---
## Section 10.1.6: AUSM+up (granular)

**Summary:** A modified version of the AUSM+up flux scheme, developed by Houim and Oran (2016), includes flux limiters for packing events. It defines the flux of conservative variables (mass, momentum, thermal energy, and granular energy) across a face with specific formulas.

**Content:**
A modified version of the AUSM+up flux scheme has been developed by Houim and Oran (2016) and includes flux limiters in the event of packing. The flux of conservative variables, (mass, momentum, thermal energy, and granular energy) across a face is given by

$$F^{AUSM+up}_s = F_p + \dot{m} \begin{cases} U_{own} & \text{if } \dot{m} > 0 \\ U_{nei} & \text{if } \dot{m} \le 0 \end{cases} \quad (10.29)$$

where

$$\dot{m} = F c_{1/2} M_{1/2} \begin{cases} \alpha_{own}\rho_{own} & \text{if } M_{1/2} > 0 \\ \alpha_{nei}\rho_{nei} & \text{if } M_{1/2} \le 0 \end{cases} \quad (10.30)$$

$$F = \frac{(c_{1/2} - \epsilon)[1 + |M_{1/2}|(1 - G/2)]}{2} \frac{\max(\alpha_{s,own}, \alpha_{s,nei})}{\alpha_{s,max}} [\alpha_{own}\rho_{own} - \alpha_{nei}\rho_{nei}] \quad (10.31)$$

and Fp = (0, Ptot,1/2I, 0, 0), with αs being the total granular volume fraction (sum of all granular phases), αs,max being the maximum volume fraction at which packing occurs, and epsilon being a small number (1e-10). G is defined as

$$G = \max(2(1 - D\zeta^2)) \quad (10.32)$$

and ζ is defined as

$$\zeta = \begin{cases} \frac{\alpha_s^M - \alpha_{s,crit}}{\alpha_{s,max} - \alpha_{s,crit}} & \text{if } \alpha_s^M > \alpha_{s,crit} \\ 0 & \text{if } \alpha_s^M < \alpha_{s,crit} \end{cases} \quad (10.33)$$

where αMs is the maximum granular volume fraction of the own/nei value, and αs,crit being the minimum granular volume fraction that frictional stresses occur. c1/2 is defined as

$$c_{1/2} = \sqrt{\frac{\alpha_{own}\rho_{own}c_{own}^2 + \alpha_{nei}\rho_{nei}c_{nei}^2}{\alpha_{own}\rho_{own} + \alpha_{nei}\rho_{nei}} + \epsilon} \quad (10.34)$$

The split Mach number is defined as

$$M_{1/2} = M^+_{4,own} + M^-_{4,nei} - 2\frac{K_p}{f_a}\max(1 - \sigma \bar{M}^2, 0) \frac{P_{tot,nei-}, P_{tot,own}}{(\alpha_{own}\rho_{own} + \alpha_{nei}\rho_{nei} + \epsilon)c_{1/2}^2} \quad (10.35)$$

where M±4 is calculate the same way as in the AUSM+ scheme and fa = 1. Kp, and σ are defined as

$$K_p = 0.25 + 0.75(1 - G/2) \quad (10.36)$$

$$\sigma = 0.75\sigma/2 \quad (10.37)$$

The pressure is defined as

$$P_{tot,1/2} = -K_u f_a(c_{1/2} - \epsilon)P^+_{5,own}P^-_{5,nei}(\alpha_{own}\rho_{own} + \alpha_{nei}\rho_{nei})(u_{nei} - u_{own}) + P^+_{5,own}p_{tot,own} + P^-_{5,nei}p_{tot,nei} \quad (10.38)$$

with P±5 being the same as in the AUSM+ scheme and

$$K_u = 0.75 + 0.25(1 - G/2) \quad (10.39)$$


---
## Section 10.1.3: HLLCP

**Summary:** Following the work of Xie et al. (2019), a low-speed correction has been added to the standard HLLC flux scheme. It is currently only available for single-phase simulations.

**Content:**
Following the work of Xie et al. (2019), a low-speed correction of the standard HLLC flux scheme has been added. Currently it is only available for single phase simulations.

$$F^{HLLC-P} = F^{HLLC} + \Phi^P \quad (10.9)$$

$$\Phi_p = (f - 1) \frac{S_{own}S_{nei}}{S_{nei} - S_{own}} \frac{1}{1 + |\tilde{M}|} \frac{p_{nei} - p_{own}}{\tilde{c}^2} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ \tilde{u} \\ \frac{1}{2}\tilde{u}^2 \end{pmatrix} \quad (10.10)$$

$$f = \min\left(\frac{p_{own}}{p_{nei}}, \frac{p_{nei}}{p_{own}}\right)^3 \quad (10.11)$$

where the minimum of all cell faces is used for each cell.

$$p_{**} = \theta p_* + (1 - \theta) \frac{p_{own} + p_{nei}}{2} \quad (10.12)$$

$$\theta = \min(M_*, 1) \quad (10.13)$$

$$p_{***} = f p_{**} + (1 - f)p_* \quad (10.14)$$

where p*** replaces p*K in Eq. (10.7)


---
## Section 10.1.4: AUSM+

**Summary:** The AUSM+ scheme, developed by Luo et al. (2004), constructs fluxes based on the mass flux across the face. The star state variables are written as functions of the owner and neighbor states.

**Content:**
The AUSM+ scheme was developed by Luo et al. (2004) and constructs the fluxes based on the mass flux across the face.

$$F^{AUSM+} = 0.5 [M_*c_*(U_{own} + U_{nei})] - 0.5 [|M_*|c_*(U_{own} + U_{nei})] + F_p \quad (10.15)$$

$$F_p = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \\ p_*\mathbf{n} \\ p_* \end{pmatrix} \quad (10.16)$$

The star state variables are written as

$$c_* = 0.5(c_{own} + c_{nei}) \quad (10.17)$$

$$M_* = M^+_{4,own} + M^-_{4,nei} \quad (10.18)$$

and

$$P_* = P^+_{5,own}p_{own} + P^-_{5,nei}p_{nei} \quad (10.19)$$

where

$$M^{\pm}_1(M) = 0.5(M \pm |M|) \quad (10.20)$$

$$M^{\pm}_2(M) = \begin{cases} M^{\pm}_1(M) & \text{if } |M| \ge 1 \\ \pm 0.25(M \pm 1)^2 & \text{else} \end{cases} \quad (10.21)$$

$$M^{\pm}_4(M) = \begin{cases} M^{\pm}_1(M) & \text{if } |M| \ge 1 \\ M^{\pm}_2(M)[1 \mp 16\beta M^{\mp}_2(M)] & \text{else} \end{cases} \quad (10.22)$$

$$P^{\pm}_5(M) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{M}M^{\pm}_1(M) & \text{if } |M| \ge 1 \\ \pm M^{\pm}_2(M)[(2 \mp M) - 16\alpha M M^{\mp}_2(M)] & \text{else} \end{cases} \quad (10.23)$$

with α = 3/16 and β = 1/8.


---
## Section 10.1: Riemann Solvers

**Summary:** This section describes the available Riemann solvers. For all schemes, n denotes the surface normal vector, and u represents the normal velocity component. The solutions include the volume fraction for multi-fluid problems, but these details are omitted from the descriptions, except for the AUSM+up flux scheme for granular phases.

**Content:**
The currently available Riemann solvers are described below. For all schemes, n denotes the surface normal vector, and u = u · n. There is the inclusion of volume fraction in the solution of multi-fluid problems, however with the exception of the AUSM+up flux scheme for granular phases these changes will not be included in the descriptions below.


---
## Section 10.2: Riemann advection scheme

**Summary:** This section describes an additional advection scheme using Riemann fluxes, which has been added for consistency between conserved quantities and other transport quantities like turbulence fields or species mass fractions. This allows the use of higher-order interpolation schemes for additional scalar transport, a major advantage over traditional methods that are often limited to upwinding in the presence of strong gradients. However, one limitation is that with very stiff implicit terms (e.g., in turbulence models), the explicit advection term can become negligible, leading to no advection. Therefore, implicit divergence schemes are still recommended for turbulence quantities with large implicit source terms.

**Content:**
In order to allow for consistency between the conserved quantities (mass, momentum, and energy), and additional transport quantities such as turbulence fields or species mass fractions, an additional advection scheme using the Riemann fluxes has been added. This makes use of the quantities calculated in transport of the conserved variable which then allows for the use of higher order interpolation schemes to be used for additional scalar transport. This is a major advantage over traditional methods which are generally limited to upwinding for additional transport due to presence of strong gradients.

NOTE: One limitation of using the Riemann fluxes is that, because an explicit advection term for the transported quantities is used, when very stiff implicit terms are present (such as production term in turbulence models), the advection term can become negligible leading to no advection occurring. For this reason, implicit divergence schemes are still recommended for the advection of turbulence quantities and other equations with large implicit source terms.


---
## Section 10: Flux Evaluation

**Summary:** This chapter explains that blastFoam uses explicit solutions for evolving conservative variables, which offers benefits in accuracy and computational cost. Higher-order, explicit Runge-Kutta time integration schemes improve time-integration accuracy. Stability is managed with SSP time integration methods or restrictive limiters. The calculation of conservative, hyperbolic fluxes relies on interpolated values on cell faces, found using a combination of a base interpolation scheme and flux limiters. MUSCL reconstruction is also implemented for more accurate face value reconstruction.

**Content:**
blastFoam relies on explicit solutions for the evolution of conservative variables. This has several benefits in terms of order of accuracy and computational cost. First, higher-order, explicit Runge-Kutta time integration schemes can be used which allow for higher order time-integration accuracy. Secondly, because of the required time steps of the problems of interest, iterative matrix solutions are not necessary to ensure accuracy or stability. Instead, stability can be improved through use of SSP time integraion methods or more restrictive limiters. All of the methods currently available to calculate the conservative, hyperbolic fluxes rely on the owner (own) and neighbour (nei) interpolated values on a face (also called left and right states). These interpolated values are found using a combination of a base interpolation scheme and flux limiters. OpenFOAM currently has a wide selection of available limiters such as upwind, Minmod, vanLeer, SuperBee, etc. for scalars, and upwind, MinmodV, vanLeerV, SuperBeeV, etc. for vectors; all are run-time selectable. In addition to the standard OpenFOAM interpolation schemes, MUSCL reconstruction has also been implemented. There are two main options which include upwindMUSCL and quadraticMUSCL. The upwind version uses standard upwinding for owner and neighbour interpolations. The quadratic version uses limiters in combination with the gradients and the gradient of the gradients (Hessians) to reconstruct face values. All of the standard limiters are available for MUSCL reconstruction. The vector limiters (MinmodV for example) are not used with the MUSCL schemes since the limiting is done for each component of the variables. An example of the fvSchemes dictionary is shown below


---
## Section 10.1.1: HLL

**Summary:** The HLL scheme, based on Toro et al. (1994), neglects the contact wave. The fluxes are determined based on the wave propagation speeds, which are approximated from the owner and neighbor states using Roe averages.

**Content:**
The HLL scheme is based on Toro et al. (1994) in which the contact wave is neglected. The fluxes take the form:

$$F^{HLL} = \begin{cases} F_{own} & \text{if } 0 \le S_{own} \\ \frac{S_{nei}F_{own} - S_{own}F_{nei} + S_{own}S_{nei}(U_{nei} - U_{own})}{S_{nei} - S_{own}} & \text{if } S_{own} \le 0 \le S_{nei} \\ F_{nei} & \text{if } 0 \ge S_{nei} \end{cases} \quad (10.1)$$

The owner and neighbor states to approximate the wave propagation speeds are defined as

$$S_{own} = \min(u_{own} - c_{own}, \tilde{u} - \tilde{c}) \quad (10.2)$$

$$S_{nei} = \max(u_{nei} + c_{nei}, \tilde{u} + \tilde{c}) \quad (10.3)$$

where uK is the normal flux of the respective state, cK is the speed of sound, and u˜ and c˜ are the Roe averages velocities and speed of sounds, respectively.


---
## Section 16.6: initializeAtmosphere

**Summary:** The 'initializeAtmosphere' utility is used to set up stable initial pressure and density fields by balancing the hydrostatic pressure gradient with gravitational forces. It is a modified version of the standard OpenFOAM utility and offers two models: 'hydrostatic' and 'tabulated'. The hydrostatic model dynamically solves for the pressure gradient and adjusts the density, while the tabulated model uses a predefined list of pressures and temperatures versus height. An optional hydrostatic correction can be applied after using the tabulated data. Inputs can be provided via a dictionary or command line, and a 'system/atmosphereProperties' file is required for the tabulated option.

**Content:**
The initializeAtmosphere utility is used to initialize stable pressure and density fields such that the hydrostatic pressure gradient and gravitational acceleration force are balanced. The hydrostatic initialization procedure is a modified version of that available in OpenFOAM (OpenCFD Ltd., 2018a). Currently, there are two available models: hydrostatic and tabulated. The hydrostatic model dynamically solves for the hydrostatic pressure gradient and adjusts the density accordingly. The tabulated model reads a list of pressures and temperatures versus height to determine the density fields. An optional hydrostatic correction can be used once the table values have been set. See tutorials/initializeAtmosphere for an example. Inputs can either be read from a dictionary or through the command line. If the tabulated option is requested, system/atmosphereProperies must be available.
Usage: initializeAtmosphere [OPTIONS]

**Parameters Table:**
[
  {
    "Variable": "-pRef",
    "Description": "Reference pressure [Pa]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "-hRef",
    "Description": "Reference height, or the height at the lowest point of the mesh [m]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "-phase",
    "Description": "Name of the phase to set (only for multiphase)"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "-zone",
    "Description": "Optional cellZone to set (all cells by default)"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "-type",
    "Description": "Optional override of the model. (hydrostatic by default, no default if read from dictionary)"
  }
]


---
## Section 5.1.11: JANAF

**Summary:** The JANAF thermodynamic model represents enthalpy using a temperature-based polynomial (OpenCFD Ltd., 2018b). It requires seven coefficients for both low and high temperature states, as well as high, low, and switching temperatures. Currently, it is only available for ideal gases.

**Content:**
The JANAF thermodynamic model can be used in which the enthalpy is represented by a temperature based polynomial (OpenCFD Ltd., 2018b). Seven coefficients must be specified for both the low temperature and high temperature states, as well as the high, low, and switching temperatures. Currently, this model is only available for ideal gases.

**Parameters Table:**
[
  {
    "Variable": "highCpCoeffs",
    "Description": "High enthalpy coefficients"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "lowCpCoeffs",
    "Description": "Low enthalpy coefficients"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "Tlow",
    "Description": "Minimum temperature [K]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "Tcommon",
    "Description": "Switching temperature [K]"
  },
  {
    "Variable": "Thigh",
    "Description": "Maximum temperature [K]"
  }
]
